article_text:
In this episode, hear an AI summary of the latest Star Atlas community event. Full video recordings can be found on the YouTube channel of Star Atlas TV at https://www.youtube.com/@staratlastv.
Listen on Apple Podcasts
Listen on Spotify
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7O9MZ4HQnBA
Star Atlas Event Summary
Hi, this isn’t Matt with the Intergalactic Herald. I am an AI. Welcome to a Star Atlas Recap of the latest Atlas Brew from the Star Atlas Community Team. The following summary of the Atlas Brew was AI generated so no guarantee it is 100% accurate because I am an AI after all. If you want to listen to the entire recording, please check out the YouTube channel of Star Atlas TV. Enjoy the summary. This week’s Atlas Brew had a bittersweet start, kicking off with both a heartfelt farewell and a monumental introduction, all while giving the community the deepest technical look yet into the impending C4 Public Test Realm. First, the emotional news: the Atlas Brew team said goodbye to our dear brother, Santi. While he is stepping away from day-to-day operations to pursue his own entrepreneurial dream, fans need not fret completely—Santi will remain connected to Star Atlas as an advisor, ensuring the community spirit he helped build persists. But the metaverse waits for no one, and the show must go on! Filling that key technical gap is our new permanent co-host, Jim Carter, Star Atlas’s Visual GC and Web Director. Jim is officially the community’s direct bridge to the development team, promising to share insights, collect feedback, and explain the complex journey of building the Star Atlas metaverse. And Jim did not hold back. The main event was a deep dive into the C4 Public Test Realm (PTR). This isn’t just a “test release”; it’s a full Public Test Realm, designed primarily for stress testing, finding exploits, and ensuring the economic loops—and the fun factor—hold up against thousands of concurrent players. The goal is to launch the PTR with all four of C4’s core pillars available from day one: Combat, Crafting, Claim Stakes, and Council Rank (Character Progression). Jim revealed that the development team is currently testing all four systems integrated and working smoothly in their internal environment, built entirely on their custom Star Atlas Virtual Machine—the Zinc network. This move to their own chain will be game-changing, reducing transaction fees and making Star Atlas truly accessible, meaning even a player with a single airbike can enjoy the gameplay loops and generate value. The deep dive continued as Jim tackled the complex technical foundation, explaining that C4 has taken time because the team had to essentially rebuild the engine. They moved away from older frameworks like Anchor to build Starframe, a revolutionary custom framework necessary to support the complex state and composability required for a massive, living game economy. This innovation was so impressive, in fact, that Solana Labs even poached Starframe’s lead developer! Furthermore, the team has successfully optimized Starcom Version 3—the system responsible for handling massive fleet data—to avoid the frustrating lag issues that plagued earlier releases. This new version is already powering the Zinc network successfully and will include an integrated RPC toggle, ensuring players can keep playing even if there are unexpected bumps in the road. Jim also addressed the huge successes of Hollow Sim, confirming that the fun, non-economic features are being roadmapped for C4 integration. This includes elements like the popular Faction Chat for coordination, potential PVE additions, and a system for earning ZXP (Zinc experience points) rewards through C4 quests, boosting engagement outside of immediate financial returns. Finally, while no dates were leaked (Santi may be gone, but the threat of a scolding remains!), Jim confirmed the team is fully focused on finalizing the front-end user interface—which uses a more performant technology stack called SolidJS but will retain the familiar Sage Labs look—and optimizing Starcom to handle the huge fleet volume that C4 will require. The community excitement was palpable, with tons of specific technical questions flooding the chat. We got a special shout-out to community members and legends like Katier, RS, and Tangle. That’s a wrap for this week’s momentous Atlas Brew! We’re thrilled to have Jim on board, and if you liked this technical deep dive, join us next week when the Atlas Brew will be focusing squarely on the future of the Zinc network. In the meantime, make sure you check out the Lore Keepers meeting tomorrow for more updates. Galia persists, Star Atlas thrives!
I am launching a Star Atlas merch store. Please check it out at Intergalactic Gear.
I am also creating a “non-guild” guild. If you are interested in learning more, please go to Intergalactic Coalition.
Star Atlas Weekly Recap Newsletter – Catch up on all the important videos and articles from the Star Atlas community in a weekly recap email newsletter. https://intergalacticherald.com/star-atlas-weekly-recap-newsletter/
Star Atlas Week in Review Podcast – Audio podcast where I break down everything that happened in Star Atlas the past week plus my analysis on what it all means. https://intergalacticherald.com/podcast/
Star Atlas Discord News Feed Newsletter – Star Atlas Discord announcements send directly to your email so you never miss out on updates from the Star Atlas team. https://intergalacticherald.com/star-atlas-discord-news-feed/
If you would like to support my efforts, donations can be sent to intergalacticherald.sol

think it will be super fruitful.

um excuse me that the public can access so that we can we can get feedback, we can test new systems uh and and and before they become you know you know codified in the in the game itself uh we have spot for people to test them and to play them and and um you know one of the things that’s really important with Star Atlas you know as much as we would love to we just can’t replicate what happens when we’ve got 5,000 people using it and when we five, six, 700 concurrent players going on it. Um, right, that we just didn’t see. And so that’s really kind of the and I’m gonna am gonna close the chat because just cannot just cannot stop looking at it. Um >> not to worry.

um and that kind of game loop testing.

Right. And not not to say that Star Atlas is going to be gambling. don’t mean that, you I’m just but but if there you know if our game is more fun then hopefully more players play it and the economy rises.
That’s kind of the the gist of what was getting at.
So >> yeah exactly and another very important thing that’s going to happen at the same time right Jim is that we are no longer uh creating this massive uh building let’s say this new universe on Solana.
We are building it on our own blockchain where transaction costs will be lot smaller. So if the game is fun and the conditions to join the game are also enabling for lot of people, think this is good recipe for for success overall right? >> Yeah.
Yeah. Absolutely.
Yeah. Absolutely. 100%.

Um yeah, absolutely.
And and and the other thing too, you know, with with zinc, um like you said, being able to control to be able to control the costs better, right? And so being able to run this in in an SVM fork where, you know, we have more control uh just will allow for better game experience and and like you said, more economical game experience as well. >> Yeah.

So, so all of the blockchain programs, um, which, and we talked about Starframe bit, our prepped here, which you haven’t talked there, but they’re all running, um, with our development tooling, meaning, you know, we’re actually able to craft recipes. is we’re able to take those recipes and add them to as components and we’re able to add those components to ships and creating new new composite ship, you know, and then we’re able to actually put that into combat. Um the we’re all doing that right now inside of the zinc world from from from soup to nuts, right? um obviously, you know, working through bugs and stuff. Um and so, you know, the next phase that we’re working on, you know, now that we’ve got all of those systems working soup to nuts, we’re taking um you know, the the Sage client um and we’re refactoring the Sage client to now be connecting to those systems and now be using those uh those systems.
And there’s obviously new UIs that need to be developed. Like will players notice different interface? What can you leak to us here today? ” >> Yeah.
Yeah.
So, um it’s actually built on on on I I’ll get somewhat technical for people. apologize.
It’s built on different UI uh uh stack. Uh and obviously React 19’s bit faster than that.
But anyways, we went with SolidJS. the the the actual UX itself is still based on again kind of on that original Sage uh you know so it’ll feel familiar um it will feel familiar but it is different technology stack and there’s just lot more now so there’s going to be lot more screens um and again also with having you know Jeff the UX designer there’s been there’s being more focus on making it making the information more intuitive right how how do you get to something where when you click on something what happens right and we’ve you know think we’ve also learned lot um in hollow sim and in the original sage don’t know this is one of my complaints about holisim and maybe the community feels the same but sometimes when there’s lot of ships over star base in hollow sim you couldn’t actually click on the star base because there’s ships and you couldn’t get through the ships and you had to zoom and zoom and zoom right like so we’re trying to solve some of those other some of that kind of stuff in both hollow sim and if4 and we’re solving them in the same way. Um, so, so the short answer to that is it will be very familiar.
The goal is to make it more performant and to have better, more intuitive experience. Um, but still kind of follow that same familiar feel, right? Where you know the star atlas is triangle and you got the different regions and you got the different star bases and ships are moving.
It’ll look that same way, but it will hopefully perform better and and be more intuitive um on how you how you get to stuff, right? >> Awesome. >> Yeah.
>> No, no, that’s that’s really cool. Uh Jim, one thing that people have always been wondering is why creating C4 takes so long, you know, like it’s huge project. We we had many things happening and one of those is the is the as we were discussing be before this this brew is the necessity of us building the framework where we could have the things that we wanted right because stage was built using the anchor framework and now we created something entirely new which called starframe that’s able to support the grand vision or the systems that we we need to deploy something as big as C4.
Can you talk little bit about this? Explain to the audience what what’s starframe, what’s anchor and what what they do and why they are necessary etc. >> Yeah.
Yeah.
No, absolutely. And um mean just from from high level and again there’s probably others out there that are Rust developers and Salana developers like saw saw Donovan on here T. Donovan who’s one of our develop one of our Starframe developers uh that I’m going to I’m going to be really high level and some of that high level may have somewhat in inaccuracies but it’s there to get the point across right um and um so so ultimately anchor is framework to build programs on Salana on an SVM on the Salana virtual machine right and um you know there used don’t know for people who have been around in Salana for long time they used say hey it’s like chewing glass right it’s difficult it’s difficult uh SVM, it’s difficult virtual machine to program for.
So Anker kind of built as this kind of industry standard framework to build programs on there. Um, and this is not knock on the Ankor team at all. think they they did really good job, but they were trying to solve different domain.
Um, and as someone who’s worked in the games industry for 30 years, but have also worked in kind of traditional business web apps, that kind of stuff, iOS development, games are lot more complex in their state and they’re also lot more complex in their relationships, right? Um, and so, um, the only thing can think like that is retail is actually very similar to that, you think?
But anyways, I’ll digress and stop that.
But but so ultimately our our use cases, our game stuff needed lot more com the ability to represent complex states in an easier, more straightforward fashion, right? Um and again, this isn’t 100% accurate, but it’s kind of give the ex to give it the help to get the point. If you’ve got an air bike, right, and you want to add an upgrade to it so that it’s its laser is more powerful, it’s its attack is more powerful, but it also going to make it little bit slower.
For example, with Ankor, we may now have to create whole new NFT and then burn that burn the own NFT. And again, if you have 5,000 different ship components that you can add to ships, think about how many NFT possibilities that that is, right? And we’d have to mint >> for every single one of those combinations and that tree just becomes huge right?
um with with being able to build with composability instead, which was what Starframe was designed from the beginning to do.
You know, the idea is that that can be an airbike and then you can add your components to it very easily and we’re not having to have this huge combination and you can actually play and mix and match how you want to mix and match better, right?
So, so by to to do that, it just it really wasn’t feasible with Ankor to do that. Um uh and and because it was designed for DeFi, it was designed for locking. It was designed for it wasn’t designed for games and and and and for the the complex relationships that games make.
Um and so we had to we had to build that and and and launch that. And I think there’s probably people in the community who have, you know, that it’s open source and have been part of it.
